lesson.title: Packages and Modules
lesson.summary: Organise code into reusable packages and manage dependencies with modules.

sections.heading: Packages — code organisation
sections.bodyHtml:
Every Go file starts with a package declaration. Files in the same directory belong to the same package:

// greetings/greetings.go
package greetings

func Hello(name string) string {
    return "Hello, " + name
}

Exported names start with a capital letter. Unexported (lowercase) names are package-private.

sections.heading: Importing packages
sections.bodyHtml:
Use import to use code from other packages. The import path is the module path + subdirectory:

package main

import (
    "fmt"
    "example.com/myapp/greetings"
)

func main() {
    fmt.Println(greetings.Hello("Alice"))
}

You can give imported packages an alias:

import g "example.com/myapp/greetings"
fmt.Println(g.Hello("Alice"))

sections.heading: Modules — dependency management
sections.bodyHtml:
A module is a collection of packages versioned together. The go.mod file at the root defines the module path and its dependencies:

module example.com/myapp

go 1.26

require (
    golang.org/x/text v0.14.0
)

Commands:

go mod init example.com/myapp   // create go.mod
go mod tidy                     // add missing, remove unused
go get golang.org/x/text        // add or update a dependency

sections.heading: The module cache
sections.bodyHtml:
Go stores downloaded modules in a local cache (GOMODCACHE). The go.sum file alongside go.mod pins the exact module hashes so you get the same code on every machine.

Use go mod verify to check that cached modules haven't been tampered with. Use go clean -modcache to clear the cache.
